title: SEGMENT 11 — Site-specific conjugation — engineered docking sites

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Site-specific conjugation — engineered docking sites
Let's spend a segment on conjugation chemistry, which is the chemistry of physically attaching the payload to the antibody.
This is the step that, more than any other, determines the quality and reproducibility of the ADC.

The historical approach to conjugation is what we call stochastic conjugation — meaning the chemistry happens at multiple possible sites on the antibody, more or less at random, producing the heterogeneous mixture I described in the last segment.
The newer approach, called site-specific conjugation, engineers the antibody so that payloads can only attach at defined positions.
Let me describe the two main stochastic methods first, because they're what most current ADCs use.
The first method is lysine conjugation.
Antibodies have a lot of lysine residues — typically eighty to ninety per molecule, scattered across the surface of the protein.
Lysines have a primary amine in their side chain, and primary amines react with certain electrophilic chemistry — typically activated esters like NHS esters, or related electrophiles.
So if you mix an antibody with a payload-linker construct that has an activated ester at one end, the payload will attach itself to lysines on the antibody.
But it'll attach to many different lysines, in many different combinations, producing a mixture.
Lysine conjugation is what Mylotarg used.
It's chemically simple but messy.
The second method is cysteine conjugation through interchain disulfide reduction.
An IgG antibody has four interchain disulfide bonds — two between the heavy chains in the hinge region, and one connecting each heavy chain to a light chain.
If you partially reduce the antibody with a mild reductant, you can break some of these disulfides and expose free cysteine residues.

Those cysteines have a thiol group that reacts with maleimide chemistry — typically the payload-linker has a maleimide group at one end, and it reacts with the free cysteine to form a stable thioether bond.
There are eight possible cysteine sites once you fully reduce the interchain bonds, so this gives you a DAR of up to 8, with payloads at relatively defined positions.
This is what Adcetris and T-DXd use, more or less.
Cysteine conjugation through hinge reduction is more controlled than lysine conjugation, but it's not fully site-specific.
The partial reduction gives you a distribution of zero, two, four, six, or eight payloads, depending on how many disulfides got reduced and how many cysteines got loaded.
Site-specific conjugation tries to get away from the mixture entirely.
The goal is to put payloads at exactly defined positions, on every antibody, in defined numbers.
There are several approaches.
One is engineered cysteine — you take your antibody and you mutate one or two specific positions to introduce free cysteines that are accessible to conjugation chemistry.

Genentech developed a platform called THIOMAB for this, where they identified specific positions on the antibody where a cysteine could be introduced without disrupting structure or function but would still be reactive enough for clean conjugation.
You then conjugate to just those engineered cysteines, leaving the natural disulfide bonds intact.
The result is a DAR-2 ADC with payload at exactly defined positions.
A second approach is unnatural amino acids, abbreviated ncAA or uAA.
These are amino acids that don't exist in nature — synthesized by chemists, then introduced into the protein at specific positions using an engineered translation system.

The most common ones for ADC conjugation include para-acetylphenylalanine, which has a ketone group that can be selectively conjugated through oxime chemistry, and various azide-containing amino acids that participate in click chemistry.
By incorporating an ncAA at a specific position, you create what is essentially a custom docking site — a chemical handle that nothing else in the antibody can react with.
Conjugation is then exquisitely selective.
A third approach is enzymatic conjugation — using an enzyme like transglutaminase or sortase to attach payloads at sequence-defined positions.
Transglutaminase, for example, recognizes specific glutamine residues and can be used to attach payloads via lysine-containing linkers in a one-step reaction.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold.
Stochastic conjugation is like dropping payloads onto a sticky surface — they stick wherever they land.
Site-specific conjugation is like building a docking port at a specific position on the antibody — payloads can only attach there, nowhere else.
With the dock approach, every antibody in your batch has the dock in the same place, and after conjugation, every antibody has exactly one payload at exactly that position.
Or if you build two docks, every antibody has exactly two payloads.
Defined product.
Reproducible chemistry.
Predictable behavior.
The clinical benefit of site-specific conjugation is still being established.
Several site-specific ADCs are in clinical trials.
The theoretical benefit is clear — more uniform pharmacokinetics, less risk of toxic high-DAR species, less waste from low-DAR species, easier manufacturing analytics.
The question is whether the practical benefit, in patients, is worth the extra engineering complexity.
There's also a click chemistry angle here.

Click chemistry is the name for a family of reactions — most famously the copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition, or CuAAC, and more recently the strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition, or SPAAC — that form covalent bonds with exquisite selectivity, in water, at room temperature, without side reactions.
Carolyn Bertozzi won the Nobel Prize in 2022 for the development of bioorthogonal click chemistry, and one of the major applications of that work is in site-specific ADC conjugation.
You install an azide on the antibody at a defined position, install an alkyne on the payload-linker, and then click them together.
Boom.
Defined product, every time.
Site-specific conjugation is, at this point, more of a frontier topic than a dominant production technology.
But it's almost certainly the future.
The improvement in product uniformity is so attractive, and the technologies are advancing so quickly, that within ten years most new ADCs will probably be made site-specifically.
The current generation of stochastically conjugated ADCs — including T-DXd — work well enough that the field hasn't been forced to switch.
But the next generation will get there.
